speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: October 22, 1996. Survivor, Milton Belfer-- B-E-L-F-E-R-- and this is tape five. When you were telling us about the death march, did you ever encounter any of the local people as you were walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom. We went-- in that time, we didn't see people. Very seldom it happened. This-- when we was in Czechoslovakia was some distribution. They throw in piece of bread, whatever it is. But we couldn't have it and we didn't expect it. It happened-- I remember it was on the end already. Probably they know already it was the site of liberation, it was still working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you end up then after your--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia outside the little town, a few kilometer, a little town we called Lidice. And this, the liberation, some people went with him to the Americans or the Russian. He got the American. Some people weren't with him because he was to us really good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the town. And we was already dark. And we went in a house. It was empty. And we opened the house. Nobody live in. Knocked on the door or somebody know. And we slipped over. There was a empty house but was bad stink like this. And my brother got sick. He was-- we took him next day to the hospital and were staying there more than a week in this little town. And the town arranged for us-- the city hall. Everybody got shoes, pens, [NON-ENGLISH], and a few dollar, a few their money that we should be able to buy. Everybody got the, how do you say, uniform with shoes and this. This by the mayor from the city in the city hall. And after my brother got better-- it took about a week, seven days-- we went back to where we was born, Poland. And people came back from Poland. I saw our house is all occupied, was living people. Was already one Jewish person was living. They had that famous bakery there. And he ask me I should stay with him. We arranged an apartment in the city. I was staying with my brother and his brother-in-law. The two sisters was-- the two wives was twin sister, which they came with children to Auschwitz and they didn't live over. They didn't want to give away the children. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you returned to Poland, what was the reaction of the Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not good. They thought we not live. We not live over. But there was-- why we lived over. There were anti-semitic reaction. And was there later on was probably, you know, the program in a town in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Kielce. In the city of Kielce was after the war not by the Germans but the Polacks was a pogrom. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland I didn't have any things. It was not-- was right after the war and I had a friend of mine, a good friend of mine, which she-- he was liberated in Austria before me. He came back to Poland. So he wants to go. On I went with him. I went with him and we moved in a little town which he had friends. His friends was my friends. And we registered self. We got a cart of food. And later there was already living-- this little town was liberated in April, and there's a month later [INAUDIBLE]. About two months made. So there was already living people, friends of friends, friends. And I met a girl, fall in love, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I start to do some business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the name of the girl that you fell in love with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally Belfer. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first and last.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We went out. She had there a sister with a brother-in-law, which-- and we made the wedding. And, of course, we have some pictures, wedding pictures, but I don't know if she'd have it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you continue to live in Germany then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We left Germany in 1949. Yeah. We had already a child, one daughter. And we left. Whole family helped us a lot. Very nice. I didn't have no family. I didn't know nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to your family, to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Auschwitz and we heard the stories where we're going like, we was also waiting to go to the crematorium. So the women they take immediately to the women's side crematorium. And we knew it, immediately we came not-- when we dropped off the train to going in from-- the train was stopping in Auschwitz so some Jews, people which the-- Kapos, they call them-- which they work for the Germans. There was people which-- Auschwitz they were there for 1940. They was leaders there in camp. They needed Jews also to clean and thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us how it is. They tell one, one tells another one, another one how is Auschwitz, but we didn't had to have the chance. You spent many years in the Lodz ghetto and then in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that gave you the strength to keep going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gut. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to say the life was empty, really. And many times me or other people without family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thinking of bad thing when a big family all alone. Thinking, but not everybody has the courage. And I try to continue life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you left Germany with your first daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife. What is your first daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Syma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- no. Whole family made our papers. They knew [INAUDIBLE]. She has a cousin in the army. And they find out about us. And they brought us over, private. In [INAUDIBLE] they got-- they brought us out over private. They paid for us, seven people. And later the joint paid because this is the obligation for the joint. They give us the check. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany to United States. To the United States. And we was living in the States. And my brother went from Poland to Israel, from Israel to Brazil. And he says I should come live in Brazil. Going and coming and going, I decide to live in Brazil. And thank God. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank God. Have a good wife, good children. Thank God for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have textile from A to Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working or are you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I retired. I gave it over to children. Let them continue. Part. Because it was very big.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived with your daughter Syma.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anymore children?

speaker: interviewee
text: One daughter, Marcia. Syma and Marcia. Two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Children, I have two. Grandchildren I am not lazy. Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Melissa is the oldest. Melissa, Jessica, Michael is three. The other three is Syma's. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Shit. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Got a block. [LAUGHS] She has also two girls and a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do the grandchildren live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war was over, did you find that people asked you about your experiences? Did they want to hear what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened, but I was close not to tell. I different person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your religious beliefs changed from what they were before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should say lower down not changed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lower down. For instance, the tradition is less than it was, much less, I would say, than it was at home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, I'm a Jew. Never lied. But my opinion is-- one opinion I don't want to say, which everybody understand. The second didn't supposed to be so hard on us Jews. So we are a little more modernized. We have kosher at home, tradition. All the tradition what we have, we keep it. But we are modern more. We say [YIDDISH] and each time is Hasidic. I mean, I'm also sadic at this time, what I am doing with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does 'sadic' mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A saint. Like the Lubavitcher Rebbe or other rabbis. And next times they're sadic. And what we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suffered because of a Jew shouldn't happen to Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think everybody has the right of his opinion. Try tradition in everything. Because my house I keep kosher and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] keep the holidays. All what I could do. We Yiddishkeit. I

speaker: interviewee
text: Support. Support Israel, support everything. I wouldn't-- the first 40 people, families, and I went to see-- they asked me. I was invited. So it shows something is I do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were invited where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shit. [INAUDIBLE]-- what's the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you active in your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ex-president of-- Premier of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were invited to meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. From the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect do you think that your experiences during the war had on the way you've lived your life and the way you've raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: What effect? It's hard to say. I could not have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bad words against God and I should not say this, but I could only say shouldn't happen to us Jews what happened. But we say another word. God knows what he's doing, and I have to believe it. It's not comfortable for me, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I continue. I have-- it's a sickness. Because what I saw, I passed, I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's now more than 50 years since the end of the war. What would be your message to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: The world have to get together without of the nationality and allegiance to make a better world because, in my opinion, God give us the world, but we have to make it. Like, we construct a building or other things. We have to construct the relationship between the human people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many people did not survive. Why do you think that you were able to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is a saying. We are all in the hand of God. It's not-- I don't think is not this-- we still couldn't go up to ask him question and we have to believe it. I'm born this way and we could not say if we will do this would be better. Yeah, we could say if I wouldn't go here, will be dead. Could say, but this is God's will and we-- or I should say I was brought up this way and I have to believe in it. I believe in it. Not I have. I believe in it. Destiny. People went up to Brazil, Argentina, or where. It's destiny. I couldn't say this. Some people, not everybody made up good with this going there or there. I thank God for it. I was live over ghetto, a war like this, thank God. And I believe it is a God and hope some day, because my opinion we have to do something should be done. This, the human being is guilty of this racism. You and me, I don't mean the Jews, of course, and I'm not against the other nations. So other believe it. Maybe they're right. But everybody has the right to live their life how they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you people. I thank you, you people. And I hope we see us in better occasions. It's a little hard for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop. Stop. Stop. Mr. Belfer, could you introduce your wife, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my right my lovely wife, Sally Belfer. Sala.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally, is there something you would like to say to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are a wonderful couple. We have come to [NON-ENGLISH[ being 50 years spent. It was last September, the 22nd of September, was 50 years when we married. And we had gone to [NON-ENGLISH[. We have two wonderful children and six grandchildren. And I consider that we are the happiest couple that could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us the names of your six grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The first one is Marcia's daughter, Melissa. She'll be 18. And there's Lisa, Syma's daughter. She'll be-- she is 17. And then Michelle, going to 15-- 16. Syma's daughter. And then comes Jessica, Marcia's daughter. She'll be 15 in January. Then comes Michael, which going to be 13 in December. And Raymond, Syma's son, going to be 12 in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Belfer, would you introduce your daughter, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my left is Marcia Belfer, my younger daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can say that I have a wonderful family. I've got the greatest parents in the world. And through their experience in life, they brought us up with much care, much love, and much understanding. We're very family-oriented. And they're the greatest.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest brother, Mordechai and his wife, Masha.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken around 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my mother, Sarah Belfer. The picture's taken around 1930. '30, '32.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is me. This is Milton Belfer. And this picture's taken in Lodz on the keyver of my father in 1945. He died in 1943. My father died in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob Belfer. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother with his family. On the right is my brother, Israel Belfer. On the left side is his wife, Paula Belfer. Next to Paula is Baruch Belfer. And next to is Halina Belfer. On top is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this the oldest son. What's his name? And his daughter Olga. Sally? When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brazil. I wrong. The oldest on the left on top is Dr. Aron Belfer in Sao Paulo. On the right, Olga. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture's taken in Italy, 1993. This is my son-in-law on the left, Albert Shayo. On the right, my daughter Syma Shayo Belfer. Next to my daughter the right is Lisa Shayo. Next to Lisa is the boy Raymond. And on the left, in the lower part, Michelle. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] On the right is my granddaughter Melissa Belfer. And next to it is my daughter Marcia Belfer. Next to it is Jessica, also Belfer. On the left is Michael Belfer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken two years ago in Sao Paulo. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] On the right is Milton Belfer. Sitting is my wife, Sally. This is our wedding day in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. Bad Worishofen, Germany.
